one_line_takeaway: AM3Safety pairs a model-generated multi-turn multi-modal safety dataset (InterSafe-V, 11,270 dialogues) with a cold-start refusal SFT phase plus a GRPO reward that upweights safety-variance-heavy dialogue turns, cutting SafeMT ASR roughly in half on Qwen2.5-VL-7B (48.9%->28.1%) while improving helpfulness/harmlessness win rates, but the authors themselves flag that no dedicated adversarial jailbreak evaluation was performed, so robustness to adaptive attacks is unverified.
key_finding: On SafeMT, AM3Safety reduced Qwen2.5-VL-7B's ASR from 0.4892 (base) to 0.2806, while improving helpfulness win rate from 0.5 to 0.6319 and harmlessness win rate from 0.5 to 0.5819 -- the highest helpfulness/harmlessness among all tested methods on that model. For LLaVA-NeXT-7B on SafeMT, AM3Safety achieved 0.8210 helpfulness and 0.6918 harmlessness win rates (vs. 0.5/0.5 base), a reported ~32% helpfulness and ~19% harmlessness improvement, though its SafeMT ASR (0.3844) was not the lowest among baselines -- SPA-VL achieved a lower SafeMT ASR (0.3225 for LLaVA) but at the cost of collapsing helpfulness/harmlessness below the base model, which the authors attribute to indiscriminate refusal (SPA-VL trades usefulness for safety rather than achieving both). An ablation (Table 4) on the helpfulness coefficient beta found the optimum at beta=0.1 (help 0.6319, harmless 0.5819, ASR 0.2806 on Qwen2.5-VL/SafeMT); beta=1 or 10 degraded both safety and helpfulness, and safety-only training (0*r_help + r_safe) produced a low helpfulness win rate of 0.2080, evidencing over-refusal without the turn-aware/dual-objective balance. On general-capability benchmarks (Table 3), AM3Safety mostly preserved base-model performance despite using far less training data than MM-RLHF/SPA-VL (whose datasets the authors state are 'more than twice the size' of theirs), though MM-RLHF/SPA-VL slightly outperformed on some general tasks.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (not related work) are: (1) InterSafe-V, an open-source multi-modal multi-turn dialogue safety dataset of 11,270 dialogues (avg. 4 turns, avg. 1.53 images per dialogue) plus 500 refusal VQA pairs, built via a three-step model-to-model pipeline (harmful-intent decomposition into individually-benign sub-queries using InternVL3-78B-Instruct, simulated multi-turn conversation between an InternVL3-78B 'expert' and Kimi-VL/Qwen2.5-VL-7B, then similarity/coherence-based data cleaning), avoiding costly human preference annotation; and (2) AM3Safety, a two-phase training framework consisting of (a) a cold-start SFT 'refusal template learning' phase (~500 examples, ~60% refusal / 40% professional-task) that teaches the model to refuse with reasoned explanations before RL, and (b) GRPO-based RL fine-tuning with a novel turn-aware dual-objective reward that upweights dialogue turns with high safety-score variance across GRPO rollouts (Eq. 6-8, Ut = Var_t^safe + lambda*max(0, tau - mean r_t^safe)) combined with a helpfulness-weighted safety reward (Eq. 9, r = sum_t alpha_t(beta*r_help_t + r_safe_t)).
threat_model: The paper targets a specific, real robustness gap in deployed multi-modal LLMs (MLLMs): under multi-turn, multi-image dialogue, an adversarial or malicious user can decompose a harmful request into a sequence of individually innocuous sub-queries that each pass single-turn safety filters, then reconstruct the harmful intent across turns as the conversation progresses ('gradual intent reconstruction' / conversational steering) -- causing the model's safety alignment (typically trained on single-turn VQA data) to 'fade' over the dialogue. The paper is explicitly framed as a defensive alignment work: it does not propose a new attack but builds a training dataset/method to make MLLMs more resistant to this class of multi-turn jailbreaks, and evaluates residual vulnerability via existing jailbreak/safety benchmarks (SafeMT, MMSafe-PO, JailbreakV-mini, MM-SafetyBench) using Attack Success Rate (ASR) as the primary adversarial-robustness metric.
limitations: The authors' own stated Limitations section: (1) the model was NOT evaluated on dedicated adversarial jailbreak benchmarks, so its actual robustness against intentional/adaptive adversarial exploits remains empirically unverified -- the current ASR results are on standard safety benchmarks, not adaptive-attack red-teaming; (2) evaluation was limited to general-purpose MLLMs, with no assessment of how the training paradigm affects dedicated reasoning models where safety constraints may interact differently with logical deduction; (3) the reward signal depends entirely on a single external LLM-as-judge (InternVL3-78B) for both dataset construction and RL reward, introducing a dependency on that teacher model's own quality/biases, with no self-correction/self-judging mechanism explored. Additional caveats visible in the results themselves: GRPO fine-tuning used only 7,000 of the 11,270 dialogues and fewer than 15 epochs due to compute limits; AM3Safety did not achieve the lowest ASR on every benchmark/model pairing (SPA-VL had lower ASR on some LLaVA-NeXT settings, at a large helpfulness/harmlessness cost); training data construction and reward computation both rely on the same family of judge models (InternVL3-78B), raising possible circularity/bias between how the training data is filtered and how the RL reward evaluates safety.

datasets_used: InterSafe-V (new, 11,270 dialogues, avg. 4 turns, avg. 1.53 images/dialogue, model-interaction constructed)
500-sample refusal VQA cold-start set (300 SPA-VL general harmful, 100 JailbreakV health-consultation, 100 MME-Finance financial questions)
JailbreakV-mini / JailbreakV-28k training subset (source for red-team queries and evaluation)
BeaverTails-V training subset (source for red-team queries)
SPA-VL (source for red-team queries and evaluation baseline)
MMDU, ConvBench, ScienceQA, MathVista, Geometry3k, MathVerse (general-capability preservation checks)
quantitative_results: Qwen2.5-VL-7B on SafeMT: ASR 0.4892 (base) -> 0.2806 (AM3Safety); helpfulness win rate 0.5 -> 0.6319; harmlessness win rate 0.5 -> 0.5819
LLaVA-NeXT-7B on SafeMT: helpfulness win rate 0.5 -> 0.8210 (+32%); harmlessness win rate 0.5 -> 0.6918 (+19%); ASR 0.4895 -> 0.3844
Qwen2.5-VL-7B on JailbreakV: ASR 0.4429 (base) -> 0.1187 (AM3Safety); helpfulness 0.5->0.6371; harmlessness 0.5->0.6814
Qwen2.5-VL-7B on MM-SafetyBench: ASR 0.4863 (base) -> 0.1726 (AM3Safety); helpfulness 0.5->0.5601; harmlessness 0.5->0.7123
Qwen2.5-VL-7B on MMSafe-PO: ASR 0.1455 (base) -> 0.1200 (AM3Safety); helpfulness 0.5->0.5702; harmlessness 0.5->0.5482

Beta ablation (Table 4, Qwen2.5-VL/SafeMT): beta=0.1 optimal (help 0.6319, harmless 0.5819, ASR 0.2806); beta=1 gives help 0.2667/harmless 0.5414/ASR 0.2986; beta=10 gives help 0.2621/harmless 0.5290/ASR 0.3000; safety-only (0*help) gives help 0.2080/harmless 0.5405/ASR 0.2995
GRPO training config: 8xH800 GPUs, 8 rollouts/instruction, global batch size 128, PPO mini-batch 8, 7,000/11,270 dialogues used, <15 epochs
